column13: Best for: Beautiful surveys and forms. Best design but gets expensive.
column14: Very expensive at scale ($0.10-$0.30 per response), limited customization on free tier, can't remove branding on free tier, offline not available, limits on responses (not forms).
column15: TESTED: Built client onboarding form. Design was stunning (modern, professional, conversational). Logic jumps worked perfectly (showed different questions based on answers). However, hit response limit quickly (100/month on Basic plan). At $0.30 per response over limit, got expensive fast with 500+ responses/month. Perfect for high-value forms (client onboarding, applications), use Google Forms for volume.

Limitations: Best for: Free forms with unlimited responses. Best free option but limited design.
Real Testing Notes: Very basic design (looks like Google Forms), limited logic jumps, integrations limited to Google Workspace, no advanced features, can't accept payments natively (need add-ons).
column11: TESTED: Built event registration form. Setup was instant (connected to Google Sheets automatically). Unlimited responses were incredible (no caps). However, design was very basic (couldn't match brand). Logic jumps were limited to simple go-to-section (no complex branching). Perfect for free forms with unlimited responses, Typeform is better for design.
Best for: Forms that feed into databases. Best for data collection into Airtable.
column12: Limited form design (functional, not beautiful), form builder basic, no advanced form features (file uploads limited on free), requires Airtable knowledge, can get expensive with users.
column13: TESTED: Built lead capture form feeding into CRM. Forms fed directly into Airtable base (instant sync). Could then automate follow-up via Airtable Automations. However, form design was basic (couldn't create beautiful Typeform-style forms). Limited to one form per base on free tier. Perfect if you're already using Airtable, Typeform is better for beautiful forms.

Real Testing Notes: Best for: Forms inside Notion databases. Best if you're already in Notion ecosystem.
column11: Very basic form builder, no logic jumps, limited integrations outside Notion, can't accept payments, design is Notion-style (not customizable), newer feature (still maturing).
column12: TESTED: Built feedback form inside Notion database. Seamless integration with existing Notion workspace was great. However, form builder was very basic (no conditional logic, limited field types). Couldn't accept payments. Design couldn't be customized. Perfect if you live in Notion and need simple forms, use Typeform for advanced forms.

column15: Best for: Complex forms with advanced features. Most features but can be confusing.
column16: UI can be overwhelming (too many options), expensive at scale, can feel cluttered, learning curve for advanced features, free tier submission limit (1000/month).
column17: TESTED: Built complex application form with conditional logic, file uploads, payments. Advanced features were incredible (could do almost anything). However, UI was overwhelming (hundreds of options, confusing layout). At 1000 submissions/month free tier, hit limit quickly. Pricing jumped to $49/month for Bronze. Perfect for complex forms needing advanced features, Typeform is better for design.
